ignore-generated-files.label: Ignore Generated Files
ignore-generated-files.summary: Write precise .gitignore rules, verify them, and keep generated output outside the maintained project history.

sketch0.title: Keep Rebuildable Output Out of History

try_ignore_generated_files_sketch0.title: Add an Ignore Rule for Build Output
try_ignore_generated_files_sketch0.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

`dist/app.js` is generated output. Write `dist/` to `.gitignore`, then use short status to confirm the generated file disappears while `.gitignore` remains visible.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_ignore_generated_files_sketch0.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: .gitignore rules keep matching untracked generated files out of normal status and staging workflows.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.

The terminal opens inside the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

`dist/app.js` is generated output. Write `dist/` to `.gitignore`, then use short status to confirm the generated file disappears while `.gitignore` remains visible.

main_sh.content:
# Use the terminal for this Git task.
# The required starting files and repository state are already prepared.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf release-kit
mkdir -p release-kit
git -C release-kit init -q -b main
git -C release-kit config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C release-kit config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd release-kit
printf %s '# Release Kit
' > README.md
cd ..
git -C release-kit add -A
git -C release-kit commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd release-kit
mkdir -p dist
printf %s 'bundled output
' > dist/app.js
cd ..

try_ignore_generated_files_sketch2.title: Stop Tracking a Local Configuration File
try_ignore_generated_files_sketch2.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

`config/local.json` was committed by mistake. The ignore rule now exists. Remove the file from Git’s index without deleting the working copy, then check status.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_ignore_generated_files_sketch2.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: An ignore rule does not automatically untrack a file; git rm --cached removes it from the index while preserving the working copy.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf release-kit
mkdir -p release-kit/config
git -C release-kit init -q -b main
git -C release-kit config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C release-kit config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
printf '%s
' '{"port":3000}' > release-kit/config/local.json
git -C release-kit add config/local.json
git -C release-kit commit -q -m "Add local configuration"
printf '%s
' 'config/local.json' > release-kit/.gitignore
git -C release-kit add .gitignore
git -C release-kit commit -q -m "Ignore local configuration"

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf release-kit
mkdir -p release-kit/config
git -C release-kit init -q -b main
git -C release-kit config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C release-kit config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
printf '%s
' '{"port":3000}' > release-kit/config/local.json
git -C release-kit add config/local.json
git -C release-kit commit -q -m "Add local configuration"
printf '%s
' 'config/local.json' > release-kit/.gitignore
git -C release-kit add .gitignore
git -C release-kit commit -q -m "Ignore local configuration"

try_ignore_generated_files_sketch3.prompt:

`.gitignore` is staged with rules for `dist/` and `*.log`. Commit it as `Ignore generated output`, then verify that build and log files remain ignored.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_ignore_generated_files_sketch3.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: Committing .gitignore shares the project policy while ignored generated files remain outside history.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

try_ignore_generated_files_sketch3.starterCode:
# Use the terminal for this Git task.
# The required starting files and repository state are already prepared.
try_ignore_generated_files_sketch3.solutionCode:
git commit -m "Ignore generated output"
git check-ignore dist/app.js debug.log
git status --short

*.log

printf %s 'local log
' > debug.log

ignore-generated-files-single-choice-0.title: Purpose of .gitignore
ignore-generated-files-single-choice-0.prompt: What is the primary purpose of a `.gitignore` file in a Git repository?
ignore-generated-files-single-choice-0.hint: Think about what files you want to exclude from version control.

help.concept: The `.gitignore` file specifies which files and directories should be ignored by Git.
help.hint_1: It helps keep unnecessary files out of the repository.
help.hint_2: It prevents clutter from generated or temporary files.

options.a: To track changes in all files
options.b: To ignore specific files and directories
options.c: To delete files from the repository
options.d: To rename files in the repository

ignore-generated-files-single-choice-1.title: Effect of Adding to .gitignore
ignore-generated-files-single-choice-1.prompt: What happens when you add a file to `.gitignore` that has already been committed?
ignore-generated-files-single-choice-1.hint: Consider the difference between tracked and untracked files.

help.concept: Adding a file to `.gitignore` does not untrack it if it has already been committed.
help.hint_1: The file remains in the repository history.
help.hint_2: You need to untrack it manually if it's already committed.

options.a: The file is automatically deleted from the repository
options.b: The file is untracked and removed from history
options.c: The file remains tracked and in history
options.d: The file is renamed in the repository

ignore-generated-files-multi-choice-0.title: Common .gitignore Patterns
ignore-generated-files-multi-choice-0.prompt: Which of the following patterns are commonly used in a `.gitignore` file? Select all that apply.
ignore-generated-files-multi-choice-0.hint: Consider patterns that exclude files or directories.

help.concept: Common `.gitignore` patterns include wildcards and directory names to exclude files.
help.hint_1: Patterns often include wildcards like `*` and directory names.
help.hint_2: Look for patterns that exclude specific file types or directories.

ignore-generated-files-drag-reorder-0.title: Order of Operations for Ignoring a Committed File
ignore-generated-files-drag-reorder-0.prompt: Arrange the steps to ignore a file that has already been committed.
ignore-generated-files-drag-reorder-0.hint: Consider the sequence of adding to `.gitignore` and untracking.

help.concept: To ignore a committed file, add it to `.gitignore` and untrack it.

ignore-generated-files-fill-blank-0.title: Ignoring a Directory
ignore-generated-files-fill-blank-0.prompt: To ignore all files in the `cache/` directory, you should add `[blank1]` to your `.gitignore`.
ignore-generated-files-fill-blank-0.hint: Read the sentence around the blank and decide what role the missing word plays.

help.concept: The blank should be filled with the term that makes the statement accurate.
help.hint_1: Look at the words before and after the blank to infer what kind of term is needed.
help.hint_2: Pick the choice that completes the statement with the clearest meaning.

ignore-generated-files-fill-blank-1.title: Ignoring File Types

ignore-generated-files-sketch0.title: Keep Rebuildable Output Out of History
ignore-generated-files-sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
Generated previews, caches, and build output can usually be recreated. Recording them adds noise and merge conflicts without preserving meaningful source work.

Add a rule to `.gitignore`:

```gitignore
preview/
```

The trailing slash makes the intent clear: ignore the directory and everything beneath it. Commit the rule itself so every contributor shares the same boundary.

ignore-generated-files-sketch1.title: Prove a Rule Matches
ignore-generated-files-sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
Verify an ignore rule instead of assuming it works:

```bash
git check-ignore -v preview/index.html
```

The command prints the matching rule and file when Git ignores the path. `-v` also names the rule source, which helps debug overlapping patterns.

A silent result means no ignore rule matched.

ignore-generated-files-sketch2.title: Ignoring Is Not Untracking
ignore-generated-files-sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
`.gitignore` affects untracked paths. If a generated file is already committed, adding a rule does not remove it from history or the index.

You must first stop tracking the path while keeping the local file when that is the intended change:

```bash
git rm --cached preview/index.html
```

Review this carefully; the next commit records the path’s removal from the repository.

ignore-generated-files-sketch3.title: Prefer Narrow Rules
ignore-generated-files-sketch3.bodyMarkdown:
Broad patterns can hide files the project actually needs. Prefer `preview/` over `*.html` when only one generated directory should be excluded.

Use status and `git check-ignore -v` together. Status confirms ignored output stays out of the change list; check-ignore proves why.

A good ignore file is small, intentional, and tied to the project’s real generated artifacts.
